title: Energy Efficiency and Conservation Master Plan up to 2030
description:
The Energy Efficiency and Conservation Master Plan is a supreme plan of Bangladesh’s initiative on energy efficiency and conservation, of which preparation requirement is stipulated in the Energy Efficiency and Conservation Rules (2014). The Plan declares Bangladesh's unyielding commitment for EE&C implementation
 
This master plan is developed along 7 policy axes
 
  1. Energy Management Program for large industrial energy consumers
  2. Energy Efficiency labelling program for residential consumers based on the following sub steps:
  • Label certification / Laboratory accreditation system
  • Standardization of EE measurement method and Star Label
  • Rating criteria  Star Label Standardization (Unification)
  • Participation of manufactures, importers and retail shops (mandatory/voluntary)
  • MEPS (Minimum Energy Performance Standard)
  1. Energy efficiency building program based on the Bangladesh National Building Code (BNBC)
  2. Energy efficiency & conservation finance program for private companies
  3. Government own initiatives (Green Purchase Program for Eco-friendly public procurement and obtaining some ISO certifications)
  4. Energy Consumption Data Collection (by fuel, sector, and energy intensity data)
  5. Global Warming Countermeasures including the Formulation and quantification of national carbon market; a Carbon abatement project as capacity development; and  Awareness raising.

title: (Saskatchewan) Oil and Gas Emissions Management Regulations
description:
Sets mandatory methane emission reductions of 4.5 million tonnes CO2e annually by 2025 (this absolute number is in line with the percentage reduction target set out in the Prairie Resilience: A Made-in-Saskatchewan Climate Change Strategy). Focuses on upstream facilities.
 
As noted in the provincial methane action plan, the requirements are “results-based at the company level, not prescribed on individual facilities or pieces of equipment.” They apply to all companies whose emit at least 50,000 tonnes of CO2e per year. (Part 1 Art 3) 
 
Covered companies must “accurately measure and report” associated gas volumes at oil facilities, in tonnes CO2e. (Art. 6) The rule includes formulae for calculating emissions. (Art. 7)
 
Every year, the regulator calculates an emissions limit for each company (Art. 8). If a company calculates that they have emitted in excess of that limit (Art. 9), they must pay a penalty for each tonne emitted in excess (Art. 10).
 
Each company must also submit a methane emissions reduction plan, by 1 September 2019 or within 60 days of doing business in the province. (Art. 11) The Minister reviews each plan and approves it or requires changes, or a new plan. (Art. 12) Once an approved plan is in place, the company must comply with its plan. (Art. 13) Companies can offset their emissions with “qualifying conservation project” credits. (Part V, Art. 14)
 
The Minister may audit emissions reports from companies (Art. 15) within three years (Art. 17), and assess administrative penalties for corrections that need to be made to the registry. (Art. 16)

title: SE 154/93 on the provisions of the Environmental Management Manual of Conventional Thermal Power Plants
description:
Resolution SE 154/93 defines requirements for any power generation company to comply with air pollutant limits, notably: 
  • For oil boiler: SO2 are to be less than or equat to 1,700 mg/Nm3 and PM at 140 mg/Nm3; 
  • For gas boiler: PM emissions must be less than or equal to 6 mg/Nm3;
  • For coal boiler: SO2 emission values ​​must be less than or equal to 1,700 mg/Nm3 and PM at 120 mg/Nm3.
    Companies have the requirement to install continuous detectors for SO2, NOx and PM concentrations, with the exception of units with a capacity lower than 50 MW, for which measurements can be carried out every month.

title: Directive 2010/75/EU on industrial emissions - power generation installations limits
description:
The Industrial Emissions Directive aims to achieve a high level of protection of human health and the environment taken as a whole by reducing harmful industrial emissions across the EU. The Directive regulates over 50,000 of the EU's largest industrial installations, including energy industries, production and processing of metals, minerals and chemicals and waste management (Annex I). These installations must operate in accordance with a permit with conditions that are aligned with the principles and provisions of the Directive.

Emission limit values for combustion plants for which permit was granted before January 2013 range from: 
  • Coal, lignite and solid fuels: SO2 to be limited to 200-400 mg/Nm3 // NOx to 200-450 mg/Nm3
  • Biomass: SO2 to be limited to 200 mg/Nm3 //  NOx to 200-300 mg/Nm3
  • Peat: SO2 to be limited to 200-300 mg/Nm3 //  NOx to 200-300 mg/Nm3
  • Liquid fuels: SO2 to be limited to 200-350 mg/Nm3 //  NOx to 150-450 mg/Nm3
  • Gaseaous fuels: SO2 to be limited to 35 mg/Nm3 (with the exception of gas turbines and gas engines) //  NOx to 100-200 mg/Nm3 // CO to 100 mg/Nm3. 
For the remaining plants, emission limit values range from: 
  • Coal, lignite and solid fuels: SO2 to be limited to 150-400 mg/Nm3 // NOx to 200-450 mg/Nm3
  • Biomass: SO2 to be limited to 150-200 mg/Nm3 //  NOx to 150-250 mg/Nm3
  • Peat: SO2 to be limited to 150-300 mg/Nm3 //  NOx to 150-250 mg/Nm3
  • Liquid fuels: SO2 to be limited to 150-350 mg/Nm3 //  NOx to 100-300 mg/Nm3
  • Gaseaous fuels: SO2 to be limited to 35 mg/Nm3 (with the exception of gas turbines and gas engines) //  NOx to 50-100 mg/Nm3 // CO to 100 mg/Nm3. 

The directive was amended in 2022 to provide tighter pollutant emission limit values and extend the scope of industrial facilities.

title: Decree 13 establishing emission standards for thermoelectric plants
description:
This decree aims at providing norms and limits on air pollutants for power generation installations. 
The maximum emission limits for existing installations were: 
  • Solid fuels: SO2 to 400 mg/Nm3 // NOx to 500 mg/Nm3 // PM to 50 mg/Nm3. 
  • Liquid fuels: SO2 to 30 mg/Nm3 // NOx to 200 mg/Nm3 // PM to 30 mg/Nm3.
  • Gas: NOx to 50 mg/Nm3. 
The maximum emission limits for new installations are: 
  • Solid fuels: SO2 to 200 mg/Nm3 // NOx to 200 mg/Nm3 // PM to 30 mg/Nm3. 
  • Liquid fuels: SO2 to 10 mg/Nm3 // NOx to 120 mg/Nm3 // PM to 30 mg/Nm3.
  • Gas: NOx to 50 mg/Nm3. 
Emissions for mercury for existing and new emission sources that use coal or petcoke are limited to 0.1 mg/Nm3.

title: Ultra-Low Emission and Energy-Saving Transformation Work Plan for Coal-Fired Power Plants
description:
The Action Plan for Energy Conservation and Emission Reduction of Coal-fired Power aoùs at promoting the implmentation of "ultra-low emissions" coal power plants, with air pollutant limits for new installations to be complying with the following norms: 
  • SO2: 35 mg/m3
  • NOx: 50 mg/m3
  • PM: 10 mg/m3
  • Hg: 30 mg/m3
From 2020 onwards,  all coal-fired power plants in the country that are eligible for retrofitting shall strive to achieve the emissions limits implemented for new installations.

title: Resolution 909/2008 establishing the norms and standards for permissible emissions of pollutants into the air
description:
This regulation sets standards for air pollutants of industrial installation, including power generation. 

The standards are as follow: 
  • SO2: 550 mg/m3 for existing installations // 500  mg/m3 for new installations
  • NOx: 550 mg/m3 for existing installations // 500  mg/m3 for new installations
  • PM: 250 mg/m3 for existing installations // 150  mg/m3 for new installations
The regulation also includes norms for F gases (HF, HCI, HCT).

title: Executive Regulation N°165 For Air Quality
description:
The regulation aims at implementing the air quality rules, standards, requirements and controls set in the 2020 Environment Law. It notably sets standards for air pollutnats by industrial activities, including combustion facilities.
Installations built before September 2005 follow the below standards: 
  • SO2: 340 ng/J
  • NOx: 86 ng/J (gas fired) // 130 ng/J (oil fired)
  • PM: 13 ng/J
Installations set after September 2005 follow the below standards: 
  • SO2: 340 ng/J
  • NOx: 43 ng/J (gas fired) // 69 ng/J (oil fired)
  • PM: 13 ng/J
The above estimates relate to electric utility steam generating units operated on fossil fuel with more than 250 MBTU/h (73 MW) electrical output, but the regulation set granular differences between industrial installations.

title: Norm NS 05-062 on air pollution
description:
The aim of this regulation is set norms and standards with regards to air pollution. 
Standards for existing and new stationary installations varies with the fuel used and the generation capacity, and are displayed as follow: 
  • SO2: 2000 mg/nm3
  • NOx: 165-300 mg/nm3
  • CO: 450-650 mg/nm3
  • Dust: 50 mg/nm3

title: Standards B.E. 2566 for controlling air emissions from power plants
description:
Thailand updated its air pollutant emissions standards in 2023, with applicable norms depending on the age of the installation and its fuel.

Air emission standards for new power plants are as follows: 
  • Coal: SO2 limit to 260 mg / m3 // NOx to 180 mg / m3 // PM to 50 mg / m3 // Hg to 0.03 mg / m3
  • Gasoline:  SO2 limit to 50 mg / m3 // NOx to 130 mg / m3 // PM to 30 mg / m3 
  • Natural gas:  SO2 limit to 15 mg / m3 // NOx to 80 mg / m3 // PM to 20 mg / m3 
  • Biomass:  SO2 limit to 30 mg / m3 // NOx to 200 mg / m3 // PM to 90 mg / m3 
  • Biogas:  SO2 limit to 30 mg / m3 // NOx to 200 mg / m3 // PM to 30 mg / m3 

For power plants licensed to operate before 1996, limits for SO2 are 60-320 mg / m3 // NOx to 200-500 mg / m3 // PM to 120-180 mg / m3
For power plants licensed to operate between 1996 and 2010, limits for SO2 are 20-640 mg / m3 // NOx to 120-350 mg / m3 // PM to 60-120 mg / m3
For power plants licensed to operate between 2010 and 2023, limits for SO2 are 20-360 mg / m3 // NOx to 120-200 mg / m3 // PM to 60-120 mg / m3

title: Regulation on Control of Industrial Air Pollution
description:
The regulation aims at setting controls and standards for air pollutants from industrial installations. 

For thermal power plants with a generation capacity below 100 MW, the following limits apply: 
  •  Gas:  100 mg/nm3 for SO2 // 100 mg/nm3 for CO // 800 mg/nm3 for NOx // 10 mg/nm3 for dust
  • Coal:  200 mg/nm3 for SO2 // 100 mg/nm3 for CO // 100 mg/nm3 for dust
  • Biogas: 800 mg/nm3 for SO2 // 100 mg/nm3 for CO // 100 mg/nm3 for dust  
For thermal power plants with a generation capacity above 100 MW, the following limits apply: 
  •  Gas:  60 mg/nm3 for SO2 // 100 mg/nm3 for CO // 500 mg/nm3 for NOx // 10 mg/nm3 for dust
  • Coal:  60 mg/nm3 for SO2 // 100 mg/nm3 for CO // 500 mg/nm3 for NOx // 10 mg/nm3 for dust
  • Biogas: 800 mg/nm3 for SO2 // 100 mg/nm3 for CO // 500 mg/nm3 for NOx // 10 mg/nm3 for dust

title: Order No. 796-r implementing the national plan for emission reduction from big combustion plants
description:
The National Plan for Emission Reduction from big combustion plants sets a pathway for a gradual reduction in air pollutants from industrial installations. 
The total emission limits for all plants included in the National Plan are: 
  •  1 017 kt SO2 in 2018 to  51 kt SO2 in 2033; 
  •  191 kt NOx in 2018 to  54  kt NOx in 2033;
  •  206 kt NOx in 2018 to  5.1 kt NOx  in 2033. 
The plan sets for each of the 90 plants annual reduction objectives towards 2033.

title: Multi-Sector Air Pollutants Regulations (SOR /2016-151)
description: The Multi-Sector Air Pollutants Regulations aims at setting performance standards over air pollutants for industrial facilities.

title: Regulation on Emission of Atmospheric Pollutants from Indirect Type Boilers and Furnaces
description:
The regulation aims at setting standards and controls for air pollutants released by industrial, commercial and service activities. 

Emission limits are set for SO2, NOx and PST, depending on the fuel use for the combustion.

title: Notification No. S.O. 3305 on Thermal Power Plants Standards
description:
The regulation aims at limiting air pollutants steming from thermal power plants. 
Standards for coal based power plant's units installed before December 2003 are as follow: SO2: 200-600 mg/nm3 // NOx: 600 mg/nm3 // PM: 100 mg/nm3 // Hg: 0.03 mg/nm3. 
Standards for coal based power plant's units installed between January 2004 and December 2016 are as follow: SO2: 200-600 mg/nm3 // NOx: 300 mg/nm3 // PM: 50 mg/nm3 // Hg: 0.03 mg/nm3. 
Standards for coal based power plant's units installed from January 2017 onwards are as follow: SO2: 100 mg/nm3 // NOx: 100 mg/nm3 // PM: 30 mg/nm3 // Hg: 0.03 mg/nm3.

title: Permenlhk n°15/2019 on air quality standards for thermal power plants
description:
The regulation aims at setting rules, standards and controls for air pollutants from power generation installations. 

Standards for thermal power plants built before the application of the regulation are as follow: 
  • Coal: SO2 to 550 mg/nm3 // NOx to 550 mg/nm3 // PM to 100  mg/nm3 // Hg to 0.03 mg/nm3.
  • Diesel: SO2 to 650 mg/nm3 // NOx to 450 mg/nm3 // PM to 75  mg/nm3. 
  • Gas: SO2 to 50 mg/nm3 // NOx to 320 mg/nm3 // PM to 30  mg/nm3. 
Standards for thermal power plants built after the application of the regulation are as follow: 
  • Coal: SO2 to 200 mg/nm3 // NOx to 200 mg/nm3 // PM to 50  mg/nm3 // Hg to 0.03 mg/nm3.
  • Diesel: SO2 to 200 mg/nm3 // NOx to 250 mg/nm3 // PM to  30 mg/nm3. 
  • Gas: SO2 to 25 mg/nm3 // NOx to 100 mg/nm3 // PM to 10 mg/nm3.

title: Regulatory Measures against Air Pollutants Emitted from Factories and Business Sites
description:
The regulation aims at limiting and controling air pollutants from industrial facilities and businesses. 

Standards are set for NOx, SO2 and dust across fossil-fuel based boilers, furnaces and engines.

title: Enforcement Rules of the Air Quality Conservation Act
description:
The Enforcement Rules of the Air Quality Conservation Act aims at implementing the limits, standards and control of air pollutants in Korea. 
The rules are regularly ammended, and as of 2024, air pollution controls are set over eleven air pollutants, including NOx, SO2 and PM.

title: 2023 Emission Standards for Power Generation Units
description:
Singapore's Energy Market Authority enacted in 2023 its "Final Determination on Emission Standards Framework for New and Repowered Fossil Fuel-fired Generation Units", in order to facilitate the deployment of clean and efficient power generation units in Singapore. 
All new and repowered fossil fuel-fired plants should meet the Tier 1 emission standard of 0.355 tCO2e/MWh on an annual basis.  Plants that are unable to meet Tier 1 standard can opt to be subject to an emissions limit under Tier 2, for which  emissions allowance limit that is equivalent to a Tier 1 advanced CCGT but running at a  Plant Load Factor of 50% on an annual basis.

title: Environmental Protection and Management (Air Impurities) Regulations
description:
The regulations aim at setting limits and controls over air pollutants in Singapore. 
Standards are listed for 24 pollutants, including CO (250 mg/Nm3) and SO2 (1,700 mg/Nm3).

title: Air Quality Act
description:
The act aims at setting limits and controls over air pollutants of industrial installations in South Africa. 

The standards for newly built combustion installations are as follow: 
  • Solid fuels: SO2 to 500 mg/nm3 // NOx to 750 mg/nm3 // PM to 50 mg/nm3
  • Liquid fuels: SO2 to 500 mg/nm3 // NOx to 250 mg/nm3 // PM to 50 mg/nm3
  • Biomass: SO2 to 500 mg/nm3 // NOx to 750 mg/nm3 // PM to 50 mg/nm3
  • Gas: SO2 to 400 mg/nm3 // NOx to 50 mg/nm3 // PM to 10 mg/nm3

The standards for existing combustion installations are as follow: 
  • Solid fuels: SO2 to 3 500 mg/nm3 // NOx to 1 100 mg/nm3 // PM to 100 mg/nm3
  • Liquid fuels: SO2 to 3 500 mg/nm3 // NOx to 1 100 mg/nm3 // PM to 75 mg/nm3
  • Biomass: SO2 to 3 500 mg/nm3 // NOx to 1 100 mg/nm3 // PM to 100 mg/nm3
  • Gas: SO2 to 500 mg/nm3 // NOx to 300 mg/nm3 // PM to 10 mg/nm3

title: New Source Performance Standards: Greenhouse Gas Emissions from New, Modified, and Reconstructed Fossil Fuel-Fired Electric Generating Units
description:
The US EPA announced in April 2024 updated pollution standards for existing coal-fired and new gas-fired power plants. 

Guidelines for gas-fired power plants set two gradual phases, aiming at a large implementation of carbon capture and storage by 2032. 

Standards and guidelines for new coal-fired power plants on their end set new base load combustion turbines to have until January 1st, 2032 to meet an emission standard based on 90% capture of CO2 emissions. Existing power plants that have committed to cease operations by January 1st, 2039 will have a numeric emission rate limit based on 40% natural gas co-firing that must be met on January 1st, 2030, while those intending to run after 2039 would have to meet a numeric emission rate limit based on application of CCS with 90% capture by 2032.

title: Emissions Performance Standard Regulations
description: The Emissions Performance Standard Regulations aim at limiting carbon dioxide emissions from new fossil fuel power stations. The Emissions Limit Duty has been set at a level equivalent to emissions of 450gCO2/kWh if the plant is operating at baseload.

title: Regulation on Emission of Atmospheric Pollutants from Indirect Type Boilers and Furnaces
description:
The regulation aims at setting standards and controls for air pollutants released by industrial, commercial and service activities. 

Emission limits are set for SO2, NOx, HCl, HF, CO, and PST, depending on the fuel use for the combustion.

title: Law 4/1994 promulgating the environment law
description:
The Law sets the regulatory framework for environment protection in Egypt. 
It notably includes maximum limits of gas and air pollutants from industrial facilities, including carbon monoxide (250  mg/m3 for new facilities).

title: Decree n°18/2004 - Regulation to establish environmental quality and emission standards
description:
This decree provides air pollutants limits for industrial facilities for SOx and NOx. 
It also includes limits for vehicles with air pollutant standards for passenger cars, diesel vans, diesel trucks and two-wheelers.

title: Emission standards for Power Generation Units
description: All fossil new and fuel-fired plants should meet the Tier 1 emission standard, which is meeting emissions intensity limit of 0.355 tCO2e/MWh.  Plants that are unable to meet Tier 1 standard can opt to be subject to an emissions limit under Tier 2 - emissions allowance limit (MtCO2e cap) that is equivalent to a Tier 1 advanced CCGT but running at a PLF of 50% on an annual basis. Separately, all new and repowered plants need to be at least 30% volume hydrogen-ready with the ability to be retrofitted to become operationally 100% hydrogen-ready in the future to the extent possible.

title: The Environmental Management Act (Air quality standards)
description: The act establishes limits regarding minimum air quality standards for Large Combustion Plants over SOx and CO emissions.

title: National Environmental Quality Standards (NEQS)
description: The National Environmental Quality Standards establishes maximum level emissions for industrial and motor gaseous emissions, including SO2, NOx and CO.

title: Emission standards for pollutants from atmospheric sources
description: There are emission standards for pollutants from atmospheric stationary and mobile sources, ranging from the iron and steel to mineral wool to pharmaceutical industry.

title: CONAMA Resolution No. 436 of 12/22/2011 establishing the maximum limits for the emission of atmospheric pollutants for stationary sources
description: The resolution sets NOx and SOx maximum limits for stationary sources installed or with an application for an installation license prior to January 2, 2007.

title: Protection of the Environment Operations (Clean Air) Regulation 2022
description: This Regulation sets standards and limits for burning fuels, by prohibiting the burning of certain articles and requiring approval for certain fires and incinerators.

title: Standard NOM-085-SEMARNAT-2011 on maximum permissible emission levels from indirect heating combustion equipment
description: The standard sets maximum permissible emission levels for thermal power plants on CO, SO2 and NOx emissions. Power plans shall aim to fit these permissible emission levels no later than January 1, 2017.

title: Standards for emissions of harmful substances into the atmospheric air and harmful physical impacts on the atmospheric air
description: The Federal Law On Environmental Protection includes standards for stationary source of emissions, including SO2 and NOx.

title: Ordinance on Air Pollution Control (OAPC)
description: The Ordinance on Air Pollution Control provides standards and limits for new and existing stationary installations. It was last revised in 2024.
